title: G7 Backs Iran Deal as Hormuz Reopening Nears, Fed Holds, and Lebanon Burns
subhead: The G7 closes with unanimous support for Trump's Iran memorandum — but the deal's terms remain contested, Israel keeps striking Lebanon, oil markets brace for a slow recovery, and the Fed signals inflation worries under its new chair.

sections.heading: G7 Closes with Backing for Iran Deal, But Questions Mount
sections.body:
Leaders at the G7 summit in Evian-les-Bains threw their support behind U.S. President Donald Trump's tentative memorandum of understanding with Iran, which extends the ceasefire by 60 days and commits Tehran to reopening the Strait of Hormuz. French President Emmanuel Macron called it 'a very good deal' that ends a situation of 'great instability' with 'terrible consequences for our economies.' The G7 communique said an international maritime mission led by France and the U.K. would help secure shipping through the strait and verify mine clearance.

But the deal's text has not been publicly released. A 14-point draft circulating in media, which reportedly includes a U.S. pledge to work toward ending all American and UN sanctions on Iran and a $300 billion regional reconstruction fund, is a May-era draft and not the version actually signed, according to a regional diplomat cited by NBC News. Trump himself denied that the U.S. would invest in Iran's reconstruction: 'We are not investing 10 cents,' he said, while adding that if Iran doesn't comply, 'we'll go right back to dropping bombs.' Vice President JD Vance said mediating Qataris and Pakistanis had asked to delay releasing the full text until at least Friday's formal signing ceremony in Switzerland.
sections.whyItMatters: The G7's unanimous endorsement gives the deal international legitimacy, but the gap between leaked terms and Trump's public denials — on reconstruction funding, on sanctions timelines — creates confusion about what Iran has actually been promised and whether Washington can deliver.

sections.heading: Strait of Hormuz: Reopening Is Just the Beginning
sections.body:
The Strait of Hormuz, closed for roughly 110 days since the war began on February 28, is expected to formally reopen after Friday's signing ceremony. The IEA's monthly oil market report, released June 17, projects a 'gradual' recovery in Gulf exports and warns that global oil supply will still fall by 4 million barrels per day in 2026 before rebounding in 2027 into a significant surplus. Brent crude has dropped below $80 a barrel, its lowest since early March, on deal optimism.

But the physical recovery will lag far behind the futures market. About 500 ships and 20,000 seafarers are stranded in Gulf waters, according to the International Chamber of Shipping. Iran has designated the strait's central area as a mine danger zone, and mine clearance by French, German, and British naval vessels is still underway. Tanker tracking data shows Iraq has already raised exports to about 1 million barrels per day in the first half of June, up from roughly 98,000 bpd in May — but that's still a fraction of its pre-war 3.5 million bpd. Argus Media estimates it could take four to six months for crude export volumes to return to pre-war levels. Iraq's oil ministry says production ramp-up 'will require some time, which may vary from one field to another.'

Iran's foreign ministry said the deal would allow it to charge 'maritime service fees' for transiting ships — not 'tolls,' as Vance described the passage as 'toll-free.' This discrepancy could create complications for shipping companies, as paying fees to Iran could mean indirectly transferring funds to Iran's Revolutionary Guard, which several countries designate as a terrorist organization.
sections.whyItMatters: Markets have priced in a rapid recovery, but the physical realities — mine clearance, crew changes, depleted strategic reserves requiring restocking, and unresolved fee disputes — mean the world won't feel normal at the pump for months. The U.S. Strategic Petroleum Reserve is at its lowest since 1983.

sections.heading: Israel Defies Ceasefire Terms, Keeps Striking Lebanon
sections.body:
While the U.S.-Iran memorandum calls for an immediate end to fighting in Lebanon, Israel continues to launch airstrikes and artillery fire across the country's south. Lebanese state media reported Israeli strikes in multiple southern towns on Wednesday, while Lebanese security sources said Hezbollah launched two drone attacks on Israeli forces — attacks the group did not publicly claim. Nearly 4,000 people have been killed in Lebanon and more than 1 million displaced since Israel's invasion began on March 2.

Iran says the deal requires Israel to withdraw from Lebanon. Israel says it won't withdraw and reserves the right to respond to any Hezbollah attack. The leaked MOU text reportedly makes no mention of Israeli withdrawal. Trump, asked about Israel's actions, said he wants Israel to 'use good judgment' and reiterated that 'Israel's fighting Hezbollah too long, and too many people are being killed.' Lebanese President Joseph Aoun said Lebanon has an 'independent track' in negotiations and supports a ceasefire, welcoming Iran's help.

Netanyahu faces a different kind of pressure: with elections due by October, the Iran deal has complicated his political standing. Opposition leader Yair Lapid said Netanyahu 'lost the war' and 'did not deliver.' Reuters reports that Netanyahu's coalition is on course to lose, and Trump reportedly called Netanyahu 'fucking crazy' in a June phone call.
sections.whyItMatters: The Lebanon-Israel front is the most fragile part of the Iran deal. If Israel refuses to de-escalate, the ceasefire could unravel — and neither the U.S. nor the G7 communique specifies enforcement mechanisms beyond calling for a 'robust ceasefire' and Lebanese sovereignty.

sections.heading: Warsh's Fed Debut: Rates Held, Hawkish Pivot Signal Expected
sections.body:
New Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh's first FOMC meeting is expected to end with interest rates held steady at 3.50%–3.75%. But the real story is the likely removal of the Fed's 'easing bias' — language signaling future rate cuts — from the policy statement. Strong May retail sales data, released during the meeting, came in at nearly double economist expectations, and the unemployment rate sits at 4.3%. Inflation remains well above the Fed's 2% target, driven in part by war-related energy costs.

The updated quarterly projections are expected to show Fed officials no longer forecasting rate cuts this year, with some likely penciling in rate increases. Markets are currently pricing in a quarter-point hike by December. Warsh, who replaced Jerome Powell last month after Trump's sustained pressure on the central bank, has criticized forward guidance and said he wants Fed meetings to resemble a 'family fight.' JP Morgan's chief U.S. economist Michael Feroli wrote that Warsh may take 'a cleaver' to rate guidance language, potentially winning over the three policymakers who dissented in favor of more hawkish language at the April meeting.

The Supreme Court is also due to rule this month on whether Trump can fire Fed Governor Lisa Cook — a case with significant implications for central bank independence. Warsh has not spoken publicly about the Cook case.
sections.whyItMatters: Warsh's first meeting sets the tone for a more hawkish Fed that is less predictable and less communicative. With inflation above target and energy costs still elevated despite the Iran deal, the days of rate cuts look over for 2026 — and rate hikes are back on the table.

sections.heading: G7 on Ukraine: Stronger Words, More Air Defenses, New Sanctions on Russian Energy
sections.body:
The G7 communique reaffirmed support for Ukraine, pledged additional air defense systems, and committed to strengthening sanctions on Russia's oil and gas industries. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy, attending the summit, said the gathering 'delivered important results for Ukraine' and that partners would 'ensure support for our defence and energy resilience.' Macron called the convergence 'unprecedented,' including from Trump, whose relationship with Zelenskyy has been strained.

Trump, asked whether Putin bears more responsibility for the war's continuation, declined to comment: 'I don't want to comment on that because I'm trying to get it settled.' On potential new Russia sanctions, Trump said 'we are looking at that,' adding the U.S. would watch how far oil prices decrease — a notable caveat, since the Trump administration eased Russian oil sanctions earlier this year when Iran-related supply disruptions drove prices up.
sections.whyItMatters: The G7 delivers real commitments on air defense and sanctions, but Trump's reluctance to blame Putin and his conditional approach to Russian energy sanctions — tied to oil prices, not principle — show the limits of Western unity on Ukraine.

sections.heading: US-India Trade Deal Nears as Trump Courts Modi
sections.body:
Trump said a U.S.-India trade deal is 'very close' after meeting Prime Minister Narendra Modi at the G7. He called Modi 'a very tough negotiator' and 'the most beautiful-looking man,' and pledged that the U.S. would help India if it were attacked. Modi thanked Trump for peace efforts on Hormuz and highlighted the safety of Indian seafarers — a pointed reference, given that three Indian sailors were killed in U.S. military strikes on commercial vessels in the Strait of Hormuz just days ago.

The G7 also discussed concerns that China is flooding export markets with subsidized products, 'unfairly out-competing their own industries and destroying jobs,' as AP reported. India, South Korea, Kenya, and Brazil joined the summit as guest nations.
sections.whyItMatters: The U.S.-India trade deal and the G7's China trade concerns signal that even as the Iran war winds down, the next economic confrontation is already taking shape — between Western economies and Chinese overcapacity.

bottomLine: The G7 ended with a unified front: backing for the Iran deal, more air defenses for Ukraine, and tougher rhetoric on China's trade practices. But underneath the communique language, the cracks are visible. The Iran deal's actual terms remain secret, its enforcement mechanism for Lebanon is undefined, and Israel is already violating the ceasefire it implies. Oil markets are pricing in a fast recovery that the physical logistics of Hormuz won't deliver for months. The Fed under Warsh is shifting hawkish, and the Iran war's inflationary damage may yet force rate hikes. And on Ukraine, Trump's sanctions posture is conditional on oil prices — not conviction. The war may be winding down, but its economic and political aftershocks are just beginning.
